# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains self-reported method, architecture, case-study, benchmark-comparison, and limitation evidence about a context-engineered multi-agent coding workflow combining intent translation, external retrieval, knowledge synthesis, repository retrieval, agent orchestration, testing, and review.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Haseeb 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The proposed workflow combines four named components: GPT-5 for intent translation, Elicit for semantic retrieval, NotebookLM for document synthesis, and a Claude Code multi-agent system for code generation and validation.

## Source Excerpt

"We propose a novel context engineering workflow that combines multiple AI components—an Intent Translator (GPT-5) for clarifying user requirements, an Elicit-powered semantic literature retrieval for injecting domain knowledge, NotebookLM-based document synthesis for contextual understanding, and a Claude Code multi-agent system for code generation and validation."

## Note

The statement describes the system design as presented by the paper.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: claim-support
citation: Haseeb 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper claims the integrated workflow improves code-assistant accuracy and reliability on real-world repositories relative to baseline single-agent approaches.

## Source Excerpt

"We demonstrate that this method significantly improves the accuracy and reliability of code assistants in real-world repositories, yielding higher single-shot success rates and better adherence to project context than baseline single-agent approaches."

## Note

This is a self-reported performance claim in the abstract; the provided source text does not include full experimental tables or statistical detail.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: result
citation: Haseeb 2025, §1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In qualitative evaluation on a roughly 180K-line RainMakerz Next.js application, the system reportedly implemented complex features and bug fixes in a single generation cycle.

## Source Excerpt

"We implement this context-engineered multi-agent assistant on a real-world codebase (the RainMakerz Next.js Web application, ∼180K lines of code). In qualitative evaluations, our system can implement complex features and bug fixes in a single generation cycle."

## Note

The evidence is limited to qualitative evaluation in the paper’s described codebase.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: result
citation: Haseeb 2025, §5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Across 5 reported tasks, the multi-agent system succeeded on 4 tasks (80%) without human corrections, while the single-agent baseline succeeded on 2 tasks (40%).

## Source Excerpt

"Out of 5 tasks attempted (including feature additions and bug fixes of varying complexity), our system achieved a successful outcome (defined by passing all tests and meeting the acceptance criteria) on 4 tasks (80%) without any human corrections. The single-agent baseline succeeded on only 2 tasks (40%)."

## Note

The sample size is small and the evidence is reported by the paper authors.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Haseeb 2025, §5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The multi-agent workflow reportedly used about 30–40 messages and roughly 100k total tokens per task, compared with about 10k–20k tokens for the initial single-agent approach.

## Source Excerpt

"On average, our system exchanged around 30-40 messages across all agents for a single task and consumed roughly 100k tokens in total (input + output). In contrast, the single-agent approach might use 10k-20k tokens for a few prompt-response turns."

## Note

These are approximate usage figures reported in the paper, not independently audited measurements.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: metric
citation: Haseeb 2025, §5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper reports the multi-agent method used about 3–5× more tokens on successful tasks than the single-agent method.

## Source Excerpt

"In our evaluation, the multi-agent method used about 3–5× more tokens on successful tasks."

## Note

This adds a cost tradeoff to the reported performance gains, so the claimed improvement comes with materially higher token consumption.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: method
citation: Haseeb 2025, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The workflow begins with GPT-5 rewriting an ambiguous user request into a structured task specification with an explicit step-by-step breakdown.

## Source Excerpt

"To ensure the system accurately grasps the requirements, we employ a high-end LLM (GPT-5, in our case) to act as an Intent Translator. This agent reformulates the query into a structured specification that can guide subsequent steps." "The translator prompt is engineered to elicit a step-by-step breakdown, clarifying any implicit requirements."

## Note

This evidence describes the intended mechanism for requirement clarification.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: method
citation: Haseeb 2025, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
External knowledge retrieval uses Elicit to fetch conceptually relevant papers, documentation, or Q&A materials from key terms in the task specification, with top-k results reported as k=3–5.

## Source Excerpt

"We integrate Elicit, an LLM-driven research assistant, to perform semantic search over academic papers, documentation, and Q&A resources." "We automate this step by feeding Elicit the key terms from the task spec ... The top-k results (we used k = 3-5) are fetched as PDFs or text."

## Note

The source presents this as the retrieval procedure used in the workflow.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: method
citation: Haseeb 2025, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
NotebookLM is used to turn retrieved documents into concise summaries, table-of-contents outlines, and answers to specific implementation questions.

## Source Excerpt

"We utilize Google’s NotebookLM as a document analysis agent. All retrieved documents are provided to NotebookLM, which we prompt to produce a concise table-of-contents (TOC) summary of each and to answer specific questions."

## Note

This evidence identifies how external material is transformed into promptable context.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: method
citation: Haseeb 2025, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Repository context retrieval chunks files by function or class using tree-sitter and stores code embeddings in a vector database, with ChromaDB and Zilliz both reported as experimented backends.

## Source Excerpt

"We built a semantic code search index using a vector database (we experimented with both ChromaDB and Zilliz for this purpose)." "To preserve code structure, we chunk files by function or class definitions using an AST parser (tree-sitter)."

## Note

This is a concrete implementation detail of the code-context retrieval pipeline.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: method
citation: Haseeb 2025, §4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The system uses a centralized orchestrator-worker design in which a primary Claude instance coordinates specialist sub-agents such as frontend, backend, devops, and reviewer roles.

## Source Excerpt

"At a high level, we adopt a centralized orchestrator-worker paradigm (often called a hub-and-spoke pattern): a primary Claude instance (the 'Manager') coordinates several specialist sub-agents that each have their own context and role." "We created similar profiles for a frontend-specialist, devops-engineer, code-reviewer, etc."

## Note

This evidence describes the agent-role decomposition used by the system.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: method
citation: Haseeb 2025, §4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Each sub-agent operates with an isolated context window, while shared project knowledge is supplied through a persistent CLAUDE.md file.

## Source Excerpt

"Each subagent operates with an isolated context window." "Common background information that all agents should know ... is provided via a persistent context file (CLAUDE.md), which is preloaded into each agent’s context."

## Note

The source presents context isolation plus shared persistent context as part of the control strategy.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: incident
citation: Haseeb 2025, §5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
In the CustomBlock case study, the first generated version failed tests because the new block type had not been added to a serialization whitelist, and the system then issued a corrective backend update before passing tests on the second run.

## Source Excerpt

"After code generation, tests were executed; an initial failure occurred because the new block type was not added to a serialization whitelist. The orchestrator immediately flagged this and tasked the Backend agent with updating the serialization config. The second test run passed all unit and integration tests."

## Note

This unit includes both a concrete failure mode and the subsequent corrective action; the initial output was not fully correct on first execution.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: claim-support
citation: Haseeb 2025, §6
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper states that the approach depends heavily on retrieval quality, and an irrelevant Elicit result in one experiment introduced noise that confused the Planner agent.

## Source Excerpt

"First, the dependency on high-quality external knowledge is a double-edged sword. In one experiment, Elicit returned an irrelevant research paper due to an ambiguous query, and although NotebookLM summarized it faithfully, that summary added noise to the context and confused the Planner agent."

## Note

This is an explicit limitation showing that the retrieval-and-synthesis mechanism can degrade performance when relevance is poor.
---
version: extractor-receipt.v1
role: Extractor

evidence_count: 14
completed_at_utc: 2026-04-03T01:20:00Z

The single-agent baseline succeeded on only 2 tasks (40%)."

## Note

The sample size is small and the evidence is reported by the paper authors.
```

```markdown
---

evidence_id: evidence-13

contradiction_relevance: tension
---
In the CustomBlock case study, the first generated version failed tests because the new block type had not been added to a serialization whitelist, and the system then issued a corrective backend update before passing tests on the second run.

## Source Excerpt

"After code generation, tests were executed; an initial failure occurred because the new block type was not added to a serialization whitelist. The orchestrator immediately flagged this and tasked the Backend agent with updating the serialization config. The second test run passed all unit and integration tests."

## Note

This unit includes both a concrete failure mode and the subsequent corrective action; the initial output was not fully correct on first execution.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: claim-support
citation: Haseeb 2025, §6
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper states that the approach depends heavily on retrieval quality, and an irrelevant Elicit result in one experiment introduced noise that confused the Planner agent.

## Source Excerpt

"First, the dependency on high-quality external knowledge is a double-edged sword. In one experiment, Elicit returned an irrelevant research paper due to an ambiguous query, and although NotebookLM summarized it faithfully, that summary added noise to the context and confused the Planner agent."

## Note

This is an explicit limitation showing that the retrieval-and-synthesis mechanism can degrade performance when relevance is poor.

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains self-reported method, architecture, case-study, benchmark-comparison, and limitation evidence about a context-engineered multi-agent coding workflow combining intent translation, external retrieval, knowledge synthesis, repository retrieval, agent orchestration, testing, and review.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-018
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Haseeb 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.08322
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The proposed workflow combines four named components: GPT-5 for intent translation, Elicit for semantic retrieval, NotebookLM for document synthesis, and a Claude Code multi-agent system for code generation and validation.

## Source Excerpt

"We propose a novel context engineering workflow that combines multiple AI components—an Intent Translator (GPT-5) for clarifying user requirements, an Elicit-powered semantic literature retrieval for injecting domain knowledge, NotebookLM-based document synthesis for contextual understanding, and a Claude Code multi-agent system for code generation and validation."

## Note

The statement describes the system design as presented by the paper.
```

```markdown
---

contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper claims the integrated workflow improves code-assistant accuracy and reliability on real-world repositories relative to baseline single-agent approaches.

## Source Excerpt

"We demonstrate that this method significantly improves the accuracy and reliability of code assistants in real-world repositories, yielding higher single-shot success rates and better adherence to project context than baseline single-agent approaches."
